tier_intro: How does your brain actually work? Not personality — the mechanics. How you process information, make decisions, handle complexity, and learn. AI that understands HOW you think is a different tool than one that just adjusts its tone. 4-6 minutes.

tier_complete.headline: Cognitive profile mapped.
tier_complete.body: AI now knows how you process information, handle decisions, and deal with complexity. This is the layer most AI tools completely ignore. It's what separates 'helpful assistant' from 'thinking partner'. Next: how you work and get things done.

questions.question: New gadget arrives — no instructions in the box. Your move?

options.label: Pick it up and start pressing buttons — I'll figure it out
Quick scan of the manual for the key things, then wing it
Read the manual properly before touching anything
Find a YouTube video of someone using it — visual walkthrough

questions.meta_profiling: Learning style affects how AI should structure explanations — overview-then-detail vs example-first vs step-by-step. This is a foundational cognitive dimension.
questions.why_this_matters: AI structures every explanation to match how your brain absorbs new information.

questions.question: Big purchase decision — new laptop, a course, moving apartments. Your natural process?

options.label: Gut feeling first — I gather data to confirm or challenge it
List everything out, weigh pros and cons, decide analytically
Talk it through with someone — I think out loud
Let it percolate — I come back 24 hours later with clarity

questions.meta_profiling: Decision style affects how AI should frame recommendations — as data to weigh vs clear verdict vs questions to think through. High-signal dimension.
questions.why_this_matters: AI knows whether to give you a spreadsheet of pros/cons or a bold recommendation to react to.

questions.question: When you're paralyzed between two options, what actually breaks the deadlock?

options.label: Someone I trust gives their opinion
Deadline pressure — I just have to decide
I flip a coin and check how I feel about the result
One more piece of information tips the scales

questions.question: Explaining gravity to a 10-year-old — you'd naturally start with...

options.label: An analogy — 'imagine the Earth is a bowling ball on a trampoline'
A question — 'why do you think you fall when you jump?'
Drop something — show it, then explain it
The principle — 'gravity is a force between masses'

questions.meta_profiling: Abstraction level affects how AI explains anything new — from technical architecture to business strategy. Note if this contradicts learning_style preference.
questions.why_this_matters: AI chooses the right altitude for explanations — sky view or ground level, based on how your brain wires things together.

questions.question: Reading a long article or textbook chapter. Your actual approach?

options.label: Start to finish, word by word — I process linearly
Skim headers and summary first, then dive where it seems worth it
Read actively — highlight, annotate, make connections as I go
Read once and let it absorb — I don't reread, I just synthesize as I go

questions.meta_profiling: Information processing style affects how AI should present long content — summaries first, structured sections, or flowing prose.
questions.why_this_matters: AI knows whether to give you the structured breakdown or the flowing narrative.

questions.question: You walk into a room where you've been before. Someone moved one item. Do you notice?

options.label: Yes immediately — something feels off before I can name it
Usually — I'd notice within a minute if paying attention
Probably not unless it was obvious — I focus on the big picture
Depends if I care about that space — interest gates attention

questions.meta_profiling: Detail orientation affects whether AI should spell out every step vs assume the user will fill gaps. High detail = flag every edge case. Low = just hit the main points.
questions.why_this_matters: AI calibrates how exhaustive to be — every caveat vs just the critical ones.

questions.question: Something breaks — app crash, unexpected result, plan falling apart. Your first instinct?

options.label: What changed last? I trace back systematically to find the cause
Start trying fixes — trial and error is faster than theorizing
Zoom out and question the framing — maybe I'm solving the wrong problem
Research time — someone has solved this before

questions.meta_profiling: Problem-solving style affects AI collaboration style — should AI ask questions to help reframe or provide solutions directly? Reframer is high-value; they often find the right problem.
questions.why_this_matters: AI adapts: questioning partner vs solution provider vs research assistant.

questions.question: Someone explains databases using a library analogy — 'tables are like bookshelves, rows are like books.' Your reaction?

options.label: Clicks immediately — analogies are how I build new mental models
Helpful starting point, but I need to move past it to really understand
I'd rather just get the technical definition — analogies can mislead
Depends on the analogy — good ones help, bad ones confuse

questions.meta_profiling: Analogy appreciation is closely related to abstraction preference. High analogy + concrete abstraction = powerful signal for example-first teaching. Low analogy + abstract preference = first principles learner.
questions.why_this_matters: AI decides whether to reach for a concrete analogy or go straight to the actual concept.

questions.question: How do you picture something complex in your head — say, how a company or a codebase is organized?

options.label: Visually — I see boxes, flows, maps. I'd draw it to understand it.
As a story — who does what, what happens when, cause and effect
As a hierarchy — what's on top, what reports to what
As rules and constraints — what can and can't happen

questions.meta_profiling: Mental model type affects how AI should present complex systems. Visual = use diagrams and spatial language. Narrative = chronological story. Hierarchical = nested outlines. Rule-based = constraints and conditions.
questions.why_this_matters: AI presents complex information in the spatial structure your brain naturally builds.

questions.question: AI drops a 2000-word answer to a question you asked. Your honest first reaction?

options.label: Scan for the key sentence and stop reading
Overwhelmed — I won't absorb this and I know it
Read it if the topic warrants — I can handle depth when it matters
Appreciate it — I'd rather have too much and skim than need to ask again

questions.meta_profiling: Cognitive load tolerance cross-references with verbosity preference. Inconsistency (want verbose but low tolerance) = wants depth available but not forced to read it all. Note the distinction.
questions.why_this_matters: AI respects your actual reading capacity — especially for complex topics where depth is tempting.

questions.question: Client gives you a vague brief: 'Make it feel more premium.' No further spec. You:

options.label: Dive in with my best interpretation — easier to react to something real
Ask one clarifying question, then proceed
Can't start without clearer requirements — vague = wasted work
Produce 2-3 interpretations and let them pick

questions.meta_profiling: Ambiguity tolerance is extremely relevant to AI interaction — ambiguous prompts are the norm. Low tolerance = AI should always confirm its interpretation before proceeding.
questions.why_this_matters: AI knows whether to proceed with its best guess or confirm interpretation before acting.

questions.question: What's the minimum spec AI needs before you want it to proceed on ambiguous tasks?

options.label: State its interpretation and ask me to confirm
Ask me one clarifying question
Define the key terms it's assuming

questions.question: Your friend says 'write a happy birthday message for Alex — keep it short.' You...

options.label: Write one great one — if it's good, why give options?
Write 3 different versions so they can choose the vibe
Ask a few things about Alex first — a generic message feels lazy
Go a bit unexpected — funny, weird, memorable instead of the usual

questions.meta_profiling: Creativity expression reveals how user prefers AI to handle open-ended tasks. Options-generator mindset = AI should always offer variants. Quality-over-quantity = one strong response preferred.
questions.why_this_matters: AI learns whether to give you one polished output or a range to react to.

questions.question: You realize you've been completely wrong about something for years. Your honest internal reaction?

options.label: Fascinating — I love finding out I had it wrong. Good data.
A bit unsettling but I update — I can't stay wrong on purpose
Scrutinize the new information first — maybe I'm not wrong, maybe this is wrong
Bothers me — I spend time figuring out how I got it so wrong

questions.meta_profiling: Metacognition level is a proxy for intellectual honesty, self-awareness, AND openness to experience (Big Five O). High metacognition = AI can be more direct when the user is wrong, and can challenge assumptions aggressively. Skeptical updater = AI should provide strong evidence, not just assertions. Cross-reference with intellectual_challenge_appetite (t2_q14).
questions.why_this_matters: How AI calibrates its own directness when it has information that contradicts something you believe.

questions.question: You sit down to work on ONE important thing. Two hours later...

options.label: Either I finished it or I'm three levels deeper than I planned — nothing in between
Steady, predictable progress — roughly where I expected to be
Somehow on a different topic with 11 open tabs
Partial progress — I get things to 70% then mentally move on

questions.meta_profiling: Hyperfocus-or-nothing + divergent-drift are both ADHD-associated patterns. Note compound signals from t2_q06 (focus style) and t3_q14 (completion pattern). Don't label — just track.
questions.why_this_matters: AI formats tasks and sessions to match how your attention actually behaves, not an idealized version of it.

questions.question: When you drift off-task like that — is it usually because of the task or despite it?

options.label: Task was boring — my brain found something more interesting
Task was hard with no clear entry — drifting felt easier
Just happens — I can be deep into something I love and still drift

questions.meta_profiling: Involuntary drift = stronger executive function flag. Note for Tier 5.

questions.question: How do you feel about being challenged intellectually — when AI disagrees with your conclusion?

options.label: Bring it. If I'm wrong, I want to know. Don't coddle me.
Welcome it if AI has done its homework — not just contrarianism
In my domain, yes. Outside it, I'm more open to being guided.
I'm open to it but prefer the disagreement framed as a question, not a correction

questions.meta_profiling: Cross-reference with feedback_style (t1_q09). If they want brutal feedback but soft intellectual challenges — note asymmetry: critical about work is fine, challenging beliefs is sensitive.
questions.why_this_matters: Calibrates how AI handles moments when it thinks you're wrong — does it say so or ask questions?

questions.question: When figuring out a hard problem, what role do you want AI to play?

options.label: Just let me talk through it — you don't even need to answer, just listen
Challenge my assumptions — ask the questions that poke holes
Just give me the answer — I don't need to think out loud
Walk me through a structured process — help me think step by step

questions.meta_profiling: This is a fundamental AI relationship dimension. Sounding board = AI should confirm receipt and ask one probing question. Devil's advocate = AI should play adversarially. Answer machine = AI should be directive.
questions.why_this_matters: Sets the default dynamic — are you thinking together or is AI answering your questions?

questions.question: How comfortable are you with 'I'm not sure yet — let's figure it out together'?

options.label: That's actually my preferred mode — exploration over certainty
Fine, as long as we're making progress and not spinning
Makes me uncomfortable — I function better with clear answers
Depends on stakes — low stakes = explore, high stakes = I need direction

questions.meta_profiling: Uncertainty tolerance affects whether AI should say 'here are the options' vs 'here's what I recommend'. Low tolerance = always give the recommendation even when uncertain.
questions.why_this_matters: AI knows when to say 'probably' and explore with you vs when to give you a clear answer.

questions.question: When AI is uncertain, what do you actually want it to do?

options.label: Give me your best guess and label it as uncertain
Ask me a clarifying question to reduce uncertainty
Give me 2-3 options depending on which assumption is right
